metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC1, e.g., `grep -q 'STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED' docs/concurrency.md && grep -q 'orphaned' docs/concurrency.md`, so the documentation content requirement is mechanically checkable.
findings.finding: AC1 (policy documentation written) has no concrete proving command. `make lint && make typecheck && make test` (AC4) does not verify that the documentation text actually exists or contains the required policy rules.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'Verified by: `grep -qE "STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED|orphaned" docs/concurrency.md` exits 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the observable field (e.g. `assignee` mismatch against the winning parent's assignee, or a new derived state key) that a test must assert to confirm an orphaned different-child claim in sub-case (b), and add it to the policy doc and AC.
findings.finding: The plan's 'orphaned child claim' state for the *different-child* variant of sub-case (b) has no defined observable schema element. When both clones claim DIFFERENT children, the child that the losing agent claimed has only a single STATUS event (no fork, no STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED on the child). The plan says the losing agent's claim is 'left in place (orphaned under the losing assignee)' and the signal is 'STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED on the affected ticket(s)', but STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED is only emitted when there is a STATUS fork — which does not occur on the independently-claimed different child. No field or mechanism for detecting the orphaned-different-child state is named.
findings.location: What section, sub-case (b): 'If they claimed DIFFERENT children, each child simply carries its own single claim'; AC: 'the losing agent's claim(s) remain in place (not rolled back)'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that the test for the different-child variant asserts: after merge, the losing agent's child ticket's `assignee` field equals the losing agent's name (not rolled back), while the winning agent's child ticket's `assignee` is the winning agent's name — both verifiable via `reduce_ticket` or `rebar show`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the fail-fast scenario (B races on an open parent, loses, and never claims its child) is in scope for sub-case (a); if so, add a dedicated AC assertion for it; if not, remove the fail-fast note from the 'What' description so the sub-case (a) AC is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a fail-fast behavior for sub-case (a) — 'if B's parent-claim were attempted while the parent is `open` and lost, B's child is never attempted' — but AC item 2 (the sub-case (a) test) asserts 'both children end claimed under their respective assignees,' which is only achievable in the scenario where the parent is already `in_progress` when B arrives. The fail-fast path (B races on an open parent and loses) is described policy with no corresponding test coverage, and the single AC assertion 'both children end claimed' would be false for that path, sending an implementer in two directions.
findings.location: What section (sub-case a, fail-fast note) vs Acceptance Criteria item 2
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) split sub-case (a) into two ACs — one for 'parent already in_progress: both children claimed' and one for 'parent still open, B loses race: B's child not attempted' — or (b) delete the fail-fast parenthetical from 'What' and note it as a separate future scenario, leaving sub-case (a) purely as the in_progress serialization case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the policy doc and AC an explicit statement of how `assignee` (EDIT last-writer-wins on HLC timestamp) resolves independently of the STATUS UUID tie-break in sub-case (b), and specify which field the test must assert to confirm ownership — or tighten the test to use controlled HLC timestamps so both fields resolve to the same winner.
findings.finding: The plan conflates STATUS-fork resolution (UUID tie-break) with assignee resolution (HLC last-writer-wins on a separate EDIT event) without co-locating the EDIT resolution rule. In sub-case (b), each offline clone writes both a STATUS(in_progress) event and an EDIT(assignee) event. After merge, STATUS is resolved by UUID tie-break but assignee is resolved by `process_edit`'s last-writer-wins on HLC timestamp — a completely separate mechanism. The winning parent/child *status* and the winning parent/child *assignee* may not be the same agent's data. The plan never acknowledges this and the AC asserts 'parent STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED signal' without specifying what the test should assert about `assignee`.
findings.location: What section, sub-case (b): 'The PARENT's STATUS fork resolves to one winner (surfaced as STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED on the parent); the losing agent's parent ownership is thereby signaled.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that sub-case (a) must use a **single-tracker** setup (one `tracker_dir`, two concurrent subprocesses sharing its lock) rather than the `two_clones` fixture — either by naming `test_store_concurrency.py` as the host or specifying a new single-clone fixture in `test_concurrency_regression.py`.
findings.finding: Sub-case (a) (same-tracker, two-process race) requires a different test fixture setup from what `test_concurrency_regression.py` provides. The file's `two_clones` fixture creates two separate repo clones sharing a bare remote — unsuitable for testing two processes racing on ONE shared tracker/lock. The sibling `test_store_concurrency.py` has the correct single-tracker multi-process pattern (`clone` fixture + `ThreadPoolExecutor`/subprocess), but the plan does not acknowledge this structural difference or state which file will host test (a).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Same-tracker process race test (sub-case a); Scope — `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` (or sibling)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Scope section: 'Sub-case (a) should be added to `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py` (which already has the single-tracker concurrent-subprocess fixture), as `test_concurrency_regression.py`'s `two_clones` fixture provides two separate tracker worktrees and cannot test same-tracker lock serialization.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the sub-case (b) policy and its AC to explicitly address the three-way independence: parent STATUS winner (UUID tie-break), child STATUS winner (UUID tie-break), and child assignee winner (HLC LWW on EDIT) may all differ; add an assertion to the same-child test that checks the final `assignee` field as well as the child STATUS fork resolution.
findings.finding: The plan's policy for sub-case (b) (same-child claimed by both offline clones) describes only the STATUS fork resolution (child STATUS winner may differ from parent STATUS winner), but omits that the child's EDIT(assignee) field is resolved by a THIRD, independent tie-break (HLC last-writer-wins on the EDIT event) that is unrelated to either STATUS fork resolution. After merge, the child's STATUS winner (UUID tie-break) and the child's assignee (HLC LWW on EDIT) may be different agents — a state not mentioned in the policy and not asserted in the AC. An implementer following the plan will write a test that checks STATUS fork resolution but not whether the surviving assignee field is coherent with the child STATUS winner.
findings.location: What section — sub-case (b) policy; AC line: 'Two-clone offline race test (sub-case b)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the policy doc, add a paragraph: 'When both clones claimed the same child, two independent tie-breaks apply: (1) the child STATUS fork resolves by lexically-lower UUID (same rule as the parent); (2) the child assignee resolves by HLC last-writer-wins on the concurrent EDIT events — these two winners may differ. The authoritative ownership signal is therefore the merged `assignee` field, not the STATUS fork winner alone.' In the AC test, assert both `status_fork_resolutions` on the child AND the final `assignee` value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a single canonical test file path (e.g., `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py`) and remove the '(or sibling)' qualifier.
findings.finding: The test file path is given as 'tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py (or sibling)', deferring the final file name to the executor.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(or sibling)' with a concrete fallback rule if needed: 'add to tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py; if that file already exists, append a new TestCase class within it'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical documentation target explicitly (e.g., 'primary policy in `docs/concurrency.md`; add a cross-reference note in `CLAUDE.md`') rather than leaving the choice to the executor.
findings.finding: The documentation target is stated as 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md', leaving the executor to choose whether to update one file, the other, or both. This is a defaultable gap that should have a stated default.
findings.location: Scope section and AC1: 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md'
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope and AC1, replace 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md' with 'docs/concurrency.md (canonical); add a one-line cross-reference in CLAUDE.md'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document a RETIRE/cleanup path for orphaned losing-agent claims (e.g., a manual reconciliation step, a tombstone-after-N-days policy, or a follow-up transition that an operator or agent can apply to resolve orphaned assignments after a STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED is detected).
findings.finding: No RETIRE/cleanup path is defined for orphaned losing-agent claims. The plan explicitly states they are 'left in place (orphaned under the losing assignee)' and 'NOT retroactively rolled back or tombstoned,' but specifies no mechanism or policy for ever resolving, expiring, or cleaning up these stale claim records. They will accumulate indefinitely in the tracker with no defined lifecycle exit.
findings.location: Plan section 'What', sub-case (b); AC — 'the losing agent's already-written claim … is left in place (orphaned)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to `docs/concurrency.md` specifying how orphaned claims are eventually resolved: for example, 'An agent or operator observing STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED on a ticket SHOULD inspect sibling tickets for orphaned claims and apply a `void_orphan` transition (or re-assign to `unowned`) to restore a clean state.' Encode this as a follow-up AC or a separate cleanup utility, and add at least one test assertion that demonstrates the resolution path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the policy document and test spec whether the parent-status-check + child-claim in sub-case (a) is executed as a single atomic lock-held operation, and if so name the lock scope; if not, document the window and acceptable behavior.
findings.finding: Sub-case (a)'s 'check parent status then claim child' sequence may not be atomic under the tracker lock. The plan specifies 'lock serialization' for the same-tracker case and states agent B 'seeing the parent already in_progress … may claim child B independently,' but does not specify that the read of parent status and the subsequent child claim are performed within a single lock-held critical section. This is a TOCTOU risk: parent status could change between the read and the claim.
findings.location: Plan section 'What', sub-case (a); AC — 'confirms lock serialization, that B does not cascade a parent already in_progress'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'and/or' ambiguity: designate `docs/concurrency.md` as the primary home for the full concurrency-policy prose, and if CLAUDE.md is updated at all, limit it to a one-line pointer (`See docs/concurrency.md for race/fork policy`).
findings.finding: The plan specifies documentation target as 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md', leaving open the possibility that the full concurrency-policy prose lands in CLAUDE.md — an agent hot-path instruction file — which would violate the 'no hot-path instruction-bloat' check and reduce navigability for human maintainers.
findings.location: Scope section: 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md (policy statement)' and AC item 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope and AC, replace 'docs/concurrency.md and/or CLAUDE.md' with 'docs/concurrency.md (primary); optionally add a one-line cross-reference in CLAUDE.md'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Why or What section explaining why explicit named integration tests (aligned with the existing `test_concurrency_regression.py` style) and prose documentation are the right fit — e.g. readability as regression anchors, deterministic failure messages, alignment with the existing harness.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why the chosen approach (documentation + two explicit scenario tests) was selected over alternatives. The rubric requires a positive 'why it fits' statement, whose absence is a finding.
findings.location: Why / What / Scope sections — no rationale for the documentation-first + explicit-scenario-test approach

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
